mpEmail.subject: The Bayswater Road car park, Hyde Park — a question about Crown land
mpEmail.body:
Dear [MP_NAME],

I am writing to you about the Euro Car Parks surface car park at 150 Bayswater Road (W2 4RT), which sits on Crown land at the north-western edge of Hyde Park.

This 4,385 sqm site — larger than Granary Square at King's Cross — currently generates an estimated £0.5–1m per year in parking fees on land that Westminster residential values place at £41–64 million. Research from the Reclaim The Park campaign suggests that alternative uses could generate £8.8–217m in annual economic value and create between 150 and 2,800 jobs.

The concession agreement between The Royal Parks and Euro Car Parks is not publicly available. A Freedom of Information request has been submitted but the terms, including the expiry date, remain unknown.

I would be grateful to know:

1. Are you aware of the terms of this concession, and do you believe they represent good value for the public?
2. Would you support a public consultation on the site's future before any concession renewal?
3. Would you be willing to raise this matter with the Secretary of State for DCMS, who oversees The Royal Parks?

More information is available at reclaimthepark.org.

Thank you for your time.

Yours sincerely,
[YOUR_NAME]
[YOUR_ADDRESS]
mpEmail.instructions: Replace [MP_NAME], [YOUR_NAME], and [YOUR_ADDRESS] with your details. The email will open in your default email client.
mpEmail.apiNote: Use TheyWorkForYou API (https://www.theyworkforyou.com/api/) with getMP endpoint to look up MP by postcode. API key required (free for non-commercial use).

socialTemplates.twitter:
A 4,385 sqm car park sits on Crown land at the edge of Hyde Park. It employs 5 people.

London could have 2,800 jobs, 2.5m visitors/year, and £217m in annual economic value here instead.

The car park costs nothing to enter. It costs London everything.

reclaimthepark.org
socialTemplates.linkedin:
I've been looking at a car park on Bayswater Road at the edge of Hyde Park. It sits on Crown land worth £41–64m, generates an estimated £0.5–1m/year in parking fees, and employs about 5 people.

For comparison: Granary Square at King's Cross (smaller than this site) transformed an entire district. The Serpentine Galleries (a former tea pavilion) draws 1.2 million visitors a year.

What could this site become? A research centre, a design school, a public market, even (controversially) a tower with a free observation deck?

The Reclaim The Park campaign has published a full economic analysis with modelled alternatives. It's worth 5 minutes of your time: reclaimthepark.org
